items.original: Меня опять терзают смутные сомнения… У Шпака — магнитофон, у посла — медальон
items.translation:
I’ve got that nagging feeling again...
Shpak has a tape recorder,
and the ambassador has a medallion.
items.reference: Vague doubts haunt me once again... Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.analysis: Partly successful: the first clause conveys recurring suspicion, but it loses the mock-heroic/theatrical high register of «терзают смутные сомнения» that creates the joke. More importantly, the second clause is mistranslated as possession (“has”) rather than the intended parallel of loss/association (each has had an item involved: Shpak—tape recorder; ambassador—medallion). This weakens the logic of Bunsha’s suspicion and blunts the callback humor.

items.original: Здравствуйте, царь, очень приятно. Царь, очень приятно, царь. Очень приятно, здравствуйте, царь.
items.translation:
Hello, Tsar—pleased to meet you.
Tsar—pleased to meet you, hello.

Tsar—pleased to meet you.
Hello, Tsar—pleased to meet you!
items.reference: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.analysis: The translation preserves the key comic mechanism: repetitive, banal politeness and awkward Soviet-style greeting ritual, made absurd by the constant “Tsar” repetition. It doesn’t explicitly include the self-introduction nuance (“I’m the Tsar”), but the effect of over-polite repetition in the wrong epoch is maintained and reads naturally as drunken social babble.

items.translation: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.reference: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.analysis: Identical to the reference and fully preserves the authoritarian, mock-royal punchline.

items.original: Оставь меня, старушка, я в печали
items.translation: Leave me be, old woman—I’m in sorrow.
items.reference: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.analysis: Very strong: keeps the archaic/high-register melancholy and the insulting “старушка” contrast with the mundane setting. “Leave me be” is slightly more modern/colloquial than “Leave me,” but still works and reads well in subtitles.

items.reference: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.analysis: Meaning is conveyed (being walled in), but the English is clipped and slightly unnatural: “Bricked up” reads like a description rather than a cry of being trapped, and it weakens the melodramatic, accusatory rhythm. Also, the reference’s subject clarity (“They’ve walled me in”) better preserves the idea that “demons” are the agents; here it’s still inferable but less explicit.

items.translation: That’s what the life-giving cross can do!
items.reference: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.analysis: Accurate and functional, but slightly underplays the mock-biblical/ceremonial flourish (“Behold the power...”) that heightens the joke of religious certainty applied to a mechanical elevator. Still clearly readable and keeps the religious framing.

items.reference: The floor is open to everyone!
items.analysis: Conveys the basic command, but loses the specific emcee/MC formulaic feel of «Танцуют все!» (a set phrase announcing an open dance floor). “Everybody dance!” is understandable yet flatter and less ‘announcer-like,’ reducing the comic clash of Soviet entertainment cliché transplanted into the 16th century.

items.translation: What a perfect time to drop in!
items.reference: Now, this was a good call!
items.analysis: Good creative equivalent: keeps the self-satisfied, breezy tone of a burglar delighted by luck. It reads idiomatic and preserves the comic beat of cheerful criminal opportunism.

items.original: Икра чёрная, красная… Да! Заморская икра… баклажанная!
items.translation:
Black caviar, red caviar...
Imported caviar—eggplant!
items.reference: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.analysis: The key punchline “eggplant” is preserved, but calling it “Imported caviar—eggplant” risks the exact confusion the reference warns against: in English, “eggplant caviar” is not a familiar concept and may read as literal caviar. The joke’s inversion (cheap Soviet spread presented as exotic delicacy) is weakened without signaling “spread/dip.”

items.original: Три магнитофона, три кинокамеры, три портсигара отечественных, куртка… замшевая… Три
items.translation:
is gone: three tape recorders,
three movie cameras,

three domestic cigarette cases,
a suede jacket—three.
items.reference: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.analysis: Captures the escalating inventory and the comedic counting cadence, including the trailing “three” after the suede jacket. “Domestic cigarette cases” is a good rendering of «отечественных». Minor formatting/line-break choices slightly reduce the breathless piling-up effect, but the joke remains intact.

items.original: И тебя вылечат… и тебя тоже вылечат… и меня вылечат…
items.translation:
And they’ll cure you!

And they’ll cure you too!

And they’ll cure me!
items.reference: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.analysis: Essentially perfect: preserves the calm, rhythmic resignation and the shared-fate humor. Exclamation marks add a bit more energy than the reference’s ellipses, but the comedic effect (everyone is “mad” and will be “treated”) still lands.

summary.interpretation: 3.27 MQM penalty points per meaning unit (≈ 0.65 major errors per unit; weights: minor=1, major=5, critical=10)
